skill.name: ai-research-reproduction
skill.description: README-first AI research reproduction workflow for deep learning repositories. It selects the smallest documented inference, evaluation, or training verification target, keeps patches conservative, and records evidence in a standardized repro_outputs bundle.
skill.summary: Reproduce deep learning repositories with README-first evidence and conservative patch rules.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 178 potential issues and a 100/100 risk score, but most hits are false positives from markdown backticks, relative documentation paths, and prose. The confirmed risk is intended local command execution of README-derived reproduction commands plus local output writing; no prompt injection, network exfiltration, secret access, or malicious intent was found. Publish with a medium-risk warning that operators must review third-party README commands before enabling execution.

medium_findings.title: README-derived command execution
medium_findings.description: scripts/orchestrate_repro.py can run a command extracted from a target repository README when --run-selected is used. The implementation uses shlex.split and check=False rather than shell=True, but it still executes third-party repository instructions in the target repository directory. This is expected for a reproduction skill and should be operator-reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run call is real and the command comes from README extraction. Risk is reduced because there is no shell string execution, execution is optional, and running documented commands is the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Local orchestration subprocess calls
medium_findings.description: scripts/orchestrate_repro.py invokes neighboring helper scripts with sys.executable to scan a repository, plan setup, prepare assets, and write outputs. The script paths are constructed from the installed skill directory rather than arbitrary user input, so this is a controlled orchestration pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess usage is confirmed, but the semantic context shows fixed helper-script execution. This is a legitimate local workflow with lower injection risk than arbitrary shell execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown backtick findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings in SKILL.md, assets, and references are markdown inline code or fenced template placeholders. They document filenames, command labels, and output fields rather than executing commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown fences or inline code markers in documentation. No interpreter executes these lines as Ruby or shell backticks.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm hits do not identify active cryptographic code. The cited files contain skill metadata, prose, or ordinary string handling, and no hash algorithm is used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Inspection found metadata, prose, and asset-hint formatting at the flagged locations. There is no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic operation in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Relative reference paths are documentation, not traversal
low_findings.description: SKILL.md references ../../ shared instruction files to tell the agent which related documents to load. These are static documentation references, not runtime path concatenation or file writes outside a boundary.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The traversal-like sequences appear only in markdown references. I found no code path that accepts these strings from a user and uses them for privileged filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Temporary file use for local context transfer
low_findings.description: The script creates a temporary JSON file to pass context to a helper script, then deletes it in a finally block. This is local filesystem use and not evidence of exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file creation and cleanup are visible in one function. The remaining risk is ordinary local file exposure during execution, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance findings are reproduction status text
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hits occur in status or blocker messages that report timeouts, selected goals, or patch policy. No evidence found of collecting host inventory for exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe local run outcomes and conservative patch rules. There are no network calls or hidden collection destinations associated with these hits.

dangerous_patterns.title: README-derived command execution
dangerous_patterns.description: scripts/orchestrate_repro.py can run a command extracted from a target repository README when --run-selected is used. The implementation uses shlex.split and check=False rather than shell=True, but it still executes third-party repository instructions in the target repository directory. This is expected for a reproduction skill and should be operator-reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run call is real and the command comes from README extraction. Risk is reduced because there is no shell string execution, execution is optional, and running documented commands is the skill purpose.

content.value_statement: Deep learning reproduction is hard because commands, datasets, and assumptions are often scattered across a repository. This skill follows the README first, selects a small documented target, and records evidence in repro_outputs.
actual_capabilities: Reads a target repository README before selecting a reproduction target.
Chooses documented inference, evaluation, or training startup targets in conservative order.
Runs the selected documented command only when execution is requested.
Writes SUMMARY.md, COMMANDS.md, LOG.md, status.json, and optional PATCHES.md under repro_outputs.
Records assumptions, deviations, blockers, evidence, and human review points.
Applies a conservative patch policy focused on preserving experiment meaning.
limitations: It is intended for README-grounded AI code repositories, not generic paper summaries.
It cannot guarantee that third-party repository commands are safe to run.
Full training requires explicit confirmation and may need substantial compute.
Reproduction quality depends on the target repository documentation and available assets.

use_cases.title: Verify documented inference
use_cases.description: Run the smallest README-documented inference path and record commands, outputs, and blockers.

use_cases.title: Audit reproduction fidelity
use_cases.description: Compare README intent with executed steps, patches, and evidence before trusting a reproduction result.

use_cases.title: Standardize lab handoffs
use_cases.description: Create repeatable reproduction bundles that include assumptions, deviations, and next review points.

prompt_templates.title: Start a reproduction
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-research-reproduction on <repo_path>. Read the README first, select the smallest documented target, and write repro_outputs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new repository

prompt_templates.title: Run inference only
prompt_templates.prompt: Reproduce only the documented inference command. Do not run training. Record evidence, blockers, and deviations in repro_outputs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Limit execution to the safest target

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate with assets
prompt_templates.prompt: Reproduce the documented evaluation using assets at <asset_path>. Treat README instructions as primary and record unresolved assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Review training readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess whether documented training can start safely. Identify required datasets, checkpoints, expected duration, and human confirmations before execution.

output_examples.input: Reproduce inference for a repository at /workspace/model-repo.
output: Status: completed with recorded command output.
Evidence: README command, runtime result, and generated artifacts listed in LOG.md.
Next action: review outputs and confirm any follow-up evaluation.

output_examples.input: Reproduce training when the README only documents evaluation.
output: Selected target: documented evaluation because it is the smallest trustworthy target.
Training was not launched without confirmation.
Human decision point recorded for any full training request.
Blockers and assumptions were written to repro_outputs.

output_examples.input: Run evaluation with a local checkpoint and missing dependency notes.
output: Recorded checkpoint source as a user-provided asset.
Captured dependency assumptions and setup notes.
Reported whether README fidelity was preserved.
Listed next safe action for retry or review.

best_practices: Review README commands before enabling execution on third-party repositories.
Start with inference or evaluation before attempting training.
Keep every patch small, documented, and tied to a reproduction blocker.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill for open-ended research brainstorming without a target repository.
Do not change metrics, datasets, checkpoints, or model semantics to make a run pass.
Do not present smoke tests or startup logs as a full paper reproduction.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces a repro_outputs bundle with summary, commands, logs, status, and optional patch records.

faq.question: Does it run code from the target repository?
faq.answer: Yes, only when requested. It can run documented README commands, so users should review them first.

faq.question: Can it reproduce any paper from a PDF alone?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for repository-grounded reproduction, with the README as the primary source.

faq.question: Will it modify repository files?
faq.answer: It prefers no edits. If edits are needed, it records patch intent, verification, and README fidelity impact.

faq.question: How does it handle full training?
faq.answer: It treats full training as high cost and requires explicit user confirmation before launch.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: It is listed for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as a workflow skill.
